title: Multi-colinearity: Redundant Predictors
concept: Multicollinearity occurs when input features are highly correlated, making it difficult for the regression model to accurately calculate individual coefficients.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In multiple linear regression with three input features, what shape represents the boundary generated by the model?

options.text: A hyperplane cutting through a 4D space.
A flat 2D plane in a 3D space.
A straight line in a 2D space.

exercises.explain: Correct! A multiple regression model with three inputs uses a hyperplane to cut through the 4-dimensional space (3 inputs + 1 output).

cards.title: Double trouble
cards.body: Imagine two employees working on a project, always doing the exact same tasks together. When the project succeeds, how do you decide who deserves the credit? It's almost impossible.

cards.title: Multicollinearity
cards.body: Multicollinearity happens when two or more input features in a multiple regression model are highly correlated. They carry the same information, making their individual effects hard to isolate.

cards.title: The tandem bicycle
cards.body: Think of a tandem bike with two riders. The bike goes fast, but you can't tell who is pedaling harder because they both move together. They are redundant predictors of the bike's speed.

cards.title: Measuring in two units
cards.body: If you try to predict height using leg length in inches AND leg length in centimeters, the model will struggle. The features are perfectly correlated, confusing the weight calculations.

cards.title: Predictions still work
cards.body: You might think multicollinearity ruins the model's overall prediction. Actually, the model still predicts the output fine! It only struggles to tell you which specific feature is driving the result.

cards.title: Independent vs. Collinear Inputs
cards.body: How features relate to each other:

left.text:
Features vary separately
Model easily isolates impacts
Ideal regression setup

right.text:
Features move together
Model struggles to share credit
Redundant information

exercises.prompt: What is the main problem caused by multicollinearity in multiple regression?

options.text: The model cannot accurately calculate the individual impact (coefficient) of each feature.
The model will always output negative numbers.
The model will run out of memory during gradient descent.

exercises.explain: Correct! When features are highly correlated, the model cannot distinguish their individual contributions, leading to unstable coefficients.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Multicollinearity makes the overall predictions of the model completely inaccurate, even if we only care about the final prediction.
exercises.explain: False. Multicollinearity affects our ability to interpret individual features, but the overall predictive accuracy of the model is usually unaffected.

exercises.sentence: When features are too highly ___ with each other, we call this multicollinearity.
exercises.explain: Multicollinearity is caused by highly correlated input features that carry redundant information.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following pairs of features is most likely to cause multicollinearity?

options.text: Outdoor temperature and electricity used for heating
A house's square footage and the number of windows it has
A person's annual salary in USD and their monthly salary in USD

exercises.explain: Correct! Annual salary and monthly salary are perfectly correlated because one is simply the other multiplied by 12. This introduces perfect multicollinearity.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict student test scores. You include both 'time spent studying in hours' and 'time spent studying in minutes' as inputs. What will happen to your model?

options.text: The model will suffer from extreme multicollinearity, making it impossible to determine the individual impact of study hours versus study minutes.
The model will become twice as accurate because it has the study time measured in two different ways.
The model will fail to train because hours and minutes are measured on completely different scales.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Hours and minutes are perfectly correlated linear transformations of each other. Including both creates redundant variables and severe multicollinearity, offering zero new information while breaking the coefficient calculations. Sensational job mastering multicollinearity!
